name: MacTutor
biography:
Edmond Bour 's father was Joseph Bour and his mother was Gabrielle Jeunet. The family were not particularly well off but Edmond had a good education in Gray before going to Dijon to complete his education and to take the entrance examinations of the 횄혟cole Polytechnique.
Having been accepted for admission to the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, Bour entered in 1850 . His time there was one of the highest level of achievement and he graduated in 1852 in the first place. Bour continued his studies at the 횄혟cole des Mines in Paris and worked on a major paper Sur l'int횄짤gration des 횄짤quations diff횄짤rentielles de la m횄짤canique analytique 창혫혟 ( On the integration of the differential equations of analytical mechanics ) which was read before the Acad횄짤mie des Sciences on 5 March 1855 and published in the Journal de math횄짤matiques pures et appliqu횄짤es .
While at the 횄혟cole des Mines Bour wrote two doctoral theses. Both were on celestial mechanics, one on the three body problem and the other on the theory of attraction. Both were presented to the Facult횄짤 des Sciences in Paris on 3 December 1855 and were outstanding pieces of work. They were published twice, once in 1855 as a separate work, then again in 1856 in the Journal de l'횄혟cole Polytechnique.
Before his theses were submitted, Bour was already qualified as a mining engineer. This was in July 1855 and he was appointed as professor of mechanics and mining at the 횄혟cole des Mines in Saint-횄혟tienne. This was an appointment that he held for only a year before returning to Paris when he accepted a professorship at the 횄혟cole des Mines there. He also was appointed professor at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique. Bour had made a remarkable start to his career which was to reach even greater heights.
He received the mathematics prize of the Acad횄짤mie des Sciences in 1861 for a paper Th횄짤orie de la d횄짤formation des surfaces 창혫혟 ( Theory of deformation of surfaces ) . This work [ 1 ] :- ... in line with the analogous studies of Bonnet and Codazzi , contained several theorems on ruled surfaces and minimal surfaces; but in its printed version this work does not include the test for the integration of the problem's equations in the case of surfaces of revolution, which had enabled Bour to surpass the other competitors for the Academy's grand prize. The one disappointment in Bour's short life was his failure to win election to the Acad횄짤mie des Sciences in April 1862 when Bonnet was elected in preference to Bour. Because of this Bour turned to concentrate entirely on the mechanics course that he was teaching at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique.
Bour made many significant contributions to analysis, algebra, geometry and applied mechanics despite his early death from an incurable disease. His remarkable achievements were cut short at the age of 33 and as a consequence Bour is hardly known in the history of mathematics whereas one feels that if he had been given the chance to continue his outstanding work he would today be remembered as one of the major figures in the subject.
